no_major_event_routine_fieldwork_stead: No major event. Routine fieldwork, steady progress.
the_watershed_is_healing_across_the_bo: The watershed is healing across the board. Headwaters are cold and clean. The mainstem carries fish again. Beaver wetlands are doing the floodplain work. This is what watershed-scale recovery looks like when timing and community come together.
most_components_are_improving_a_few_st: Most components are improving. A few still need work. The trajectory is good and the community is engaged.
some_wins_some_gaps_real_watershed_wor: Some wins, some gaps. Real watershed work is rarely uniform; some pieces improved while others stalled.
average_quality_is_low_and_few_compone: Average quality is low and few components reached recovery thresholds. This is how watersheds degrade quietly when stewardship cannot keep up with pressures.
initial_conditions_look_workable_use_y: Initial conditions look workable. Use Year 1 for citizen-science monitoring and education to build community support before spending it on contested actions like dam removal.
what_you_can_do_for_this_component: 🛠 What you can do for this component
year_by_year_quality_trend_chart_by_wa: Year-by-year quality trend chart by watershed component
ai_watershed_educator_is_reading_your_: ⏳ AI watershed educator is reading your watershed data...
it_is_not_a_wabanaki_person_not_a_real: It is not a Wabanaki person, not a real watershed coordinator or hydrologist, and does not speak for any Wabanaki nation, agency, or organization.
for_authoritative_voices_on_maine_wate: For authoritative voices on Maine watershed work, consult Penobscot Nation Cultural and Historic Preservation Department, Wabanaki Public Health and Wellness, Atlantic Salmon Federation, Maine Coast Heritage Trust, Maine Audubon, Maine Soil and Water Conservation Districts, and the Maine Department of Environmental Protection.
six_watershed_components_ten_years_rea: Six watershed components, ten years, real Maine pressures: dam barriers, agricultural runoff, suburban stormwater, climate-driven floods and droughts.
hydrological_feedback_rules_tie_them_t: Hydrological feedback rules tie them together.
healthy_buffers_cool_headwater_streams: Healthy buffers cool headwater streams. Beaver wetlands attenuate floods and clean the mainstem. Low ag runoff lets the river breathe. Connected, shaded rivers bring back salmon and alewife runs.
what_if_you_had_done_nothing_for_10_ye: ↔ What if you had done nothing for 10 years?
ask_ai_watershed_educator_to_read_your: Ask AI watershed educator to read your watershed data
ai_watershed_science_educator_reads_yo: AI watershed-science educator reads your current state
no_actions_yet_this_year_pick_a_compon: No actions yet this year. Pick a component, pick a technique.
insufficient_condensation_too_warm_or_: Insufficient condensation — too warm or too dry.
slow_condensation_steady_but_gentle_pr: Slow condensation. Steady but gentle precipitation.
high_moisture_cool_temp_strong_lift_he: High moisture + cool temp + strong lift → heavy convective precipitation.
adjust_cloud_moisture_temperature_and_: Adjust cloud moisture, temperature, and wind. Widget classifies precipitation into one of four discrete types. No score, no reveal — sweep and notice.
hypothesis_free_text_what_triggers_a_s: Hypothesis (free text): What triggers a storm vs drizzle?
hold_two_sliders_steady_move_one_watch: Hold two sliders steady. Move one. Watch.
find_two_settings_producing_the_same_t: Find two settings producing the same type.
real_storms_need_both_moisture_and_ins: Real storms need both moisture and instability. Investigate.
i_understand_explain_in_own_words: I understand — explain in own words
explain_how_moisture_temperature_and_w: Explain how moisture, temperature, and wind jointly determine precipitation type.
design_note_discrete_4_type_precipitat: Design note: discrete 4-type precipitation marker; no rainfall-rate score; no reveal — by design.
water_cycle_keyboard_shortcuts_1_throu: Water Cycle. Keyboard shortcuts: 1 through 6 select a stage, J toggles Journey mode, R U P choose your journey path.
ride_one_droplet_from_the_ocean_back_t: ride one droplet from the ocean back to the ocean. You will start in the ocean, evaporate, drift into a cloud, fall as rain, then pick a path at the ground (river runoff, underground infiltration, or absorbed by a plant). Each path takes a different amount of time. Complete the loop to log a journey; 3 loops unlocks the Journey badge.
you_rode_the_same_water_molecule_the_w: You rode the SAME water molecule the whole way around — it changed form (liquid → vapor → liquid) but was never created or destroyed. Every drop you drink has been cycling for billions of years.
become_a_water_droplet_and_travel_thro: Become a water droplet and travel through the entire water cycle! Make choices at each stage and learn the science behind each transformation.
ask_a_question_e_g_why_do_clouds_float: Ask a question (e.g., Why do clouds float?)...
water_is_conserved_runoff_what_soaks_i: 💧 Water is conserved — runoff + what soaks in ≈ 100% of the rain reaching the ground. None disappears; it just takes different paths back to the sea.
